does: This paper evaluates prompt injection detection methods across diverse deployment scenarios and operating conditions. The authors develop a multi-model experimental framework comparing lexical, semantic, structural, and transformer-based detection approaches across multiple out-of-distribution settings, different data splits, and both ranking and thresholded deployment metrics. They introduce interpretable structural signals designed to capture specific attack patterns including hierarchy overrides, system prompt spoofing, role redefinition, and evasion techniques. The key finding is that detector performance varies substantially depending on the operational regime and threshold configuration, with transformer-based models generally strongest but no universal winner across all settings, while structural signals provide consistent but modest improvements over baselines.
connects: This work builds on growing research into adversarial robustness and safety of large language models, extending prior efforts to detect prompt injection attacks that attempt to override system instructions or manipulate model behavior. It responds to limitations in existing prompt injection benchmarks and evaluations that test detectors under idealized conditions misaligned with real-world deployment constraints. The paper connects to broader research on out-of-distribution detection, threshold optimization for safety-critical systems, and interpretability in machine learning by emphasizing transparent structural signals over opaque neural classifiers. It also relates to work on adversarial NLP and red-teaming that has identified classes of injection attacks, now formalized into detectable structural patterns.
enables: This deployment-aware evaluation framework enables more realistic assessment of prompt injection defenses before production deployment, highlighting the necessity of threshold tuning and regime-specific model selection. The interpretable structural signals provide a foundation for building hybrid detection systems that combine transparency with learning-based robustness. The findings suggest future work should focus on developing detectors that perform consistently across distribution shifts and operational conditions, moving beyond single-regime benchmarking. This work also opens directions for understanding failure modes of transformer-based detectors and for designing adversarially-robust injection attacks that evade structural pattern recognition, ultimately supporting safer integration of LLMs into systems where prompt injection poses genuine risks.
